full_response:
Step 1:  Start with the geometry of a counter–clockwise rotation by an angle θ in the x–y plane (about the z-axis).  
A point P with initial Cartesian coordinates  
  X = (x₀ , y₀ , z₀)ᵀ  
is taken to a new point P′ = (x′ , y′ , z′)ᵀ, where the radius r = √(x₀²+y₀²) is preserved and the polar angle in the plane increases by θ.

Step 2:  Express the primed coordinates in terms of the unprimed ones.  
In polar form, (x₀ , y₀) = (r cos φ , r sin φ). After the rotation, the new angle is φ′ = φ + θ, so

 x′ = r cos (φ+θ) = r[cos φ cos θ − sin φ sin θ]  
   = x₀ cos θ − y₀ sin θ

 y′ = r sin (φ+θ) = r[sin φ cos θ + cos φ sin θ]  
   = x₀ sin θ + y₀ cos θ

 z′ = z₀                        (no change, because the axis of rotation is the z-axis).

Step 3:  Recognize the result as a linear transformation.  
Collecting the equations,

 ⎧ x′ = cos θ · x₀ − sin θ · y₀ + 0 · z₀  
 ⎨ y′ = sin θ · x₀ + cos θ · y₀ + 0 · z₀  
 ⎩ z′ = 0     · x₀ + 0     · y₀ + 1 · z₀

these can be written compactly as the matrix product

 X′ = R[θ] · X

with

 R[θ] =  ⎡ cos θ   −sin θ    0 ⎤  
     ⎢ sin θ    cos θ    0 ⎥  
     ⎣   0        0       1 ⎦ .

The matrix R[θ] is orthogonal (RᵀR = I) and has determinant +1, confirming that it is a proper rotation matrix.

\boxed{R[\theta]=\begin{pmatrix}\cos\theta&-\sin\theta&0\\ \sin\theta&\cos\theta&0\\ 0&0&1\end{pmatrix}}, \qquad X' = R[\theta]\,X

Confidence: 92%
